title: Cat Vomiting Coffee Grounds: Causes and Emergency Care Guide
meta_desc: Vomiting liquid that looks like coffee grounds is a sign of internal bleeding in cats. Learn why this is a high-priority veterinary emergency.
content_html:
What Does Coffee Ground Vomit Mean?

If your cat is vomiting liquid that resembles dark, gritty coffee grounds, it is a sign of hematemesis, or vomiting blood. The "coffee ground" appearance occurs when blood has been present in the stomach long enough to be partially digested by stomach acid. This process causes the blood to oxidize, turning it from bright red to a dark, granular brown or black color.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

Yes. The urgency level for this symptom is High. Coffee ground emesis typically indicates significant internal bleeding in the upper gastrointestinal tract. Potential causes include gastric ulcers, the ingestion of rodenticides or other toxins, foreign body obstructions, or severe inflammatory disease. Because internal bleeding can rapidly lead to life-threatening anemia or shock, you should transport your cat to an emergency veterinary clinic immediately.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

When you arrive at the clinic, providing the veterinary team with a clear photo of the vomit can be incredibly helpful for triage. It allows the staff to confirm the presence of digested blood and assess the volume lost without you having to bring a physical sample. This visual evidence helps the veterinarian determine the level of urgency and choose the most appropriate diagnostic path for your pet.

faqs.question: Can I wait until my regular vet opens to seek help?
faqs.answer: No. Because the appearance of coffee grounds indicates internal bleeding, this cannot wait. Please contact the nearest 24-hour emergency animal hospital immediately.

faqs.question: What other symptoms should I look for?
faqs.answer: Watch for lethargy, pale gums, hidden pain, or black, tarry stools (melena), which also indicates the presence of digested blood further down the digestive tract.

faqs.question: Should I try to induce vomiting or give medication at home?
faqs.answer: Never induce vomiting or provide over-the-counter medications to a cat without a direct instruction from a veterinarian, as this can worsen internal injuries or cause further toxicity.
